topic: Area of Study 3: Developments in Instrumental Jazz 1910 to Present
subject: music

items.q: Is jazz improvisation made up with no rules?
items.correct: No - improvisers work from the tune's chord changes or modes, outlining harmonies while inventing melody in real time.
items.misconception: Jazz soloists play whatever random notes they feel like.
items.random_wrong: Jazz solos are always written out in advance by the bandleader.
items.why: Improvisation is structured invention: bebop players studied chord-scale relationships intensively, which is why solos make harmonic sense over the changes.

items.q: Does modal jazz use more chords than bebop?
items.correct: No - modal jazz uses far fewer chords, basing long stretches of improvisation on scales, as in 'So What' from 'Kind of Blue'.
items.misconception: Modal jazz is more advanced, so it must have more and faster chord changes.
items.random_wrong: Modal jazz abolishes the rhythm section entirely.
items.why: The modal innovation was harmonic slowing-down: replacing bebop's dense changes with static modes freed soloists to think melodically.

items.q: Are swing quavers notated differently from straight quavers?
items.correct: No - they are usually written as ordinary quavers, and performers apply the unequal long-short swing feel by convention.
items.misconception: If the page shows even quavers, they must be played evenly.
items.random_wrong: Swing rhythm only exists in pieces written in 6/8.
items.why: Jazz notation is a shorthand that relies on stylistic knowledge; reading it literally produces a stiff, classical delivery instead of swing.

items.q: Which Miles Davis album popularised modal jazz?
items.why: Correct answer: Kind of Blue. “Bitches Brew” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which saxophonist is most associated with the development of bebop?
items.why: Correct answer: Charlie Parker. “John Coltrane” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.random_wrong: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).. “8.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.random_wrong: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Correct answer: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.. “It has only five notes.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Head”?
items.correct: Main melodic theme of a jazz performance, played at the start and end, with improvisations on its chord changes between.
items.misconception: Style based on scales rather than chord changes, pioneered by Miles Davis on 'Kind of Blue' (1959).
items.random_wrong: Improvised chordal accompaniment provided by piano or guitar behind a soloist, supporting and responding to the improvisation.
items.why: Correct answer: Main melodic theme of a jazz performance, played at the start and end, with improvisations on its chord changes between.. “Style based on scales rather than chord changes, pioneered by Miles Davis on 'Kind of Blue' (1959).” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Modal jazz”?
items.correct: Style based on scales rather than chord changes, pioneered by Miles Davis on 'Kind of Blue' (1959).
items.misconception: Main melodic theme of a jazz performance, played at the start and end, with improvisations on its chord changes between.
items.random_wrong: Fast, virtuosic 1940s style developed by Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie, featuring complex harmony and rapid eighth-note lines.
items.why: It's Style based on scales rather than chord changes, pioneered by Miles Davis on 'Kind of Blue' (1959).. “Main melodic theme of a jazz performance, played at the start and end, with improvisations on its chord changes between.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Changes”?
items.correct: Chord progression of a tune, which improvisers outline melodically through chord-tone and scale choices.
items.misconception: Unequal subdivision of beats where eighth notes are performed with a long-short feel, fundamental to mainstream jazz.
items.random_wrong: Style based on scales rather than chord changes, pioneered by Miles Davis on 'Kind of Blue' (1959).
items.why: It's Chord progression of a tune, which improvisers outline melodically through chord-tone and scale choices.. “Unequal subdivision of beats where eighth notes are performed with a long-short feel, fundamental to mainstream jazz.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' released?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1959. “1955” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
